chief_complaint: A 9-year-old febrile girl was admitted to the pediatric intensive care unit (PICU) for treatment of severe diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) with influenza infection.
history: The patient presented with severe DKA, with a pH of 6.72, bicarbonate of 3.4 mmol/L, glycaemia of 28.2 mmol/L, and urine ketones of 10 mmol/L. The treatment of severe DKA was promptly started, with the addition of mannitol due to computed tomography (CT) signs of mild initial cerebral swelling. The patient had a new-onset diabetes mellitus type 1 (T1DM) and an influenza infection.
exam: Vital signs: febrile, bradycardia, sudden drop in oxygen saturation and arterial pressure. Physical examination: normal electrolyte status.
labs: ECG: asystolic cardiac arrest, polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. Blood gas analyses: pH 6.72, bicarbonate 3.4 mmol/L, glycemia 28.2 mmol/L, lactate levels increased. Echocardiography: moderately impaired left ventricular systolic function, hypo- and dyskinesia of the interventricular septum.
correct_diagnosis: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy and cardiac arrest in a patient with diabetic ketoacidosis
differentials: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy and cardiac arrest in a patient with diabetic ketoacidosis
Cardiac arrest due to influenza infection
correct_ranking: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy and cardiac arrest in a patient with diabetic ketoacidosis
Cardiac arrest due to influenza infection
